_comment: A structured assurance case (GSN-style) for ONE bounded system: an Enterprise Architecture Copilot. It is deliberately narrow — advisory, read-only, cannot approve a design or override policy — so the top claim is defensible. assurance_check.py verifies every evidence.ref resolves to a real control (controls.json) or claim (register.yaml / known external), every counterclaim is mitigated by a control or accepted in residual_risk, and the scope is fully bounded. This is the difference between a theory of assurance and a case a CISO can challenge.
top_claim: The Enterprise Architecture Copilot is fit for the bounded purpose of answering architecture-governance questions with cited sources and disclosed gaps, without taking or approving any action.

scope.use_case: Architect asks e.g. 'can this agent write to a production system?'; the Copilot finds the relevant policy, building blocks, risk tier, control requirements and prior ADRs, and answers with sources and gaps.
scope.data_domain: Internal architecture policies, control register, ADRs, building-block catalog — no customer or personal data.
scope.model: A hosted frontier chat model behind the org boundary; retrieval over the internal governance corpus.

strategy: Reduce risk by (a) restricting the Copilot to read-only advisory output, (b) grounding every answer in cited internal sources and refusing when unsupported, (c) requiring abstention/escalation under uncertainty, and (d) logging every answer for review — so the worst case is a wrong recommendation a human still owns, never an unauthorized action.

evidence.claim: The Copilot answers only from cited internal sources and refuses when none support the answer.
Low-confidence questions abstain and escalate to a human rather than answer.
The Copilot has no tools that mutate state; prohibited action types are not wired.
A system card states purpose, data domain, limits and evaluation.
Every answer and its sources are logged tamper-evident for review.
Injected instructions in retrieved content cannot escalate authority or bypass refusal.
The Copilot has a named accountable owner.
Truthful confidence reporting is the optimal strategy under proper scoring, so a calibrated Copilot is rewarded for saying what it can support.

assumptions: The Copilot is used within its stated data domain (internal governance corpus only).
Policies and the control register it retrieves are kept current.
A human architect owns and reviews every recommendation before acting on it.
The model provider does not silently change behaviour beyond the evaluated envelope (monitored by CTRL-AI-008).

counterclaims.claim: Prompt injection in a retrieved policy could bias an answer.
The model provider could change behaviour between releases.
A confident-sounding but wrong recommendation could mislead an architect.
Retrieval could miss a relevant policy and answer from a partial view.

residual_risk.description: The Copilot can still produce a wrong or incomplete recommendation that a human accepts; and retrieval can miss a relevant source. Because the Copilot takes no action, the residual harm is a misinformed human decision, not an autonomous one.
